sentences.text: My name is Joe Kahn, Joseph Kahn, K-A-H-N.
Kotek, my previous name was Kotek, K-O-T-E-K.
At attaining citizenship, I have changed my real name to Kahn, and there were reasons which are not important right now.
I reside at 9954 Wingtip Road in Philadelphia, the northeast part of Philadelphia.
I've been living here for 25 years in this particular house.
I've been in this country since 1950.
I arrived to the United States in May, 1950.
My birth date is January 8, 1922 and I was born in Poland.
The name of the town was Bedzin, B-E-D-Z-I-N or Bendin, as we knew it.
We knew it for Bendin.
It was a town located in the southern western part of Poland among a cluster of other big towns which were highly industralizied.
There was a lot of coal and ore mines.
Well, as a child I went to a religious school and the studies were intensive.
Coming from an orthodox upbringing, I went to an orthodox school where studies were conducted all throughout the day, beginning at 8:30 until about 8:00.
There were some secular teachings and-- and studies being conducted, but the main emphasis was on religious education.
And later on, as my father realized that I-- this is not going to be my future, he sent me to public school, where I finished public school, which is the equivalent of high school here.
And this was somewhere around 1936.
Well, the name of the school was [POLISH], which is the Polish-- the translation is public school by the name of [POLISH], S-I-E-N-K-I-E-W-I-C-Z-A.
This was the name after a Polish famous writer.
And the school was a predominantly Jewish school, even thought it was subsidized by the government, by the city, by the Polish government.

But due to the effect that Bendin or Bedzin was such a densely populated-- thickly populated Jewish-- by Jewish people, this school was as Jewish school, not by design but it just happened so that most of the citizens in this town were Jewish.
The religious school was the Agudah Israel.
Agudah, A-G-U-D-A-H-- Agudah Israel.
And again, that school was highly recognized as a very intenstive school of-- of religious studies.
How long?
About I would say-- about six years in the Agudah and about four years in the-- three or four years in the public school, from which I graduated.
My home life before the war was rather interesting in a way.

We all belonged to-- every youngster, almost everyone I knew, and there was hardly anybody that did not belong to some kind of organization, mostly Zionist organizations, from the extreme right to the extreme left.
Naturally, I belonged to the religious Zionist organizations, where others went to the other extreme.
But each youngster felt a sense of you have to belong, otherwise you were just nobody.
You were feeling a sense of identity, you were part of a movement, that you were building your hopes for the future.
And the future, naturally, was to someday immigrate to Israel.

The official language in the school was nationalist Polish and in the street people were speaking Polish, but at home, Yiddish was the mother tongue, or the [YIDDISH] as they would say in the slang Yiddish.
Yiddish was the language that we were born and raised.

So also due to the fact that our town was not far from the German border, on certain market days, the Germans from the-- from the bordering towns and villages would come on-- on market days to town so German was also heard her and there.
So it was rather a multi-language area.
After school-- after school activities were helping out my father in the workshop that we had and the living house in the house where we lived.
Coming home from school, I did my homework, and then I immediately joined my father and a few people my father employed in producing-- we produced the tops of the shoes.
That was such a-- this was a trade by itself, which today has naturally disappeared.
But at that time, this was quite a known trade.
It's like a subcontrator.
The shoe-- the shoe store owner would come to us.
We would produce the tops of the shoes, then he would take it to the shoemaker and the shoemaker would finish up the product so he can sell it in his store.
So it was quite a known profession, if I can call it as such.
But there were busy times and there were slow times.
I remember distinctly how much I worried when it was slow and I was wondering where are we going to get enough to sustain ourselves.
The family consisted of three children and two parents.
My father and my mother and I was the oldest and I had a brother that did survive, too, and I had a sister that never did survive.
My father had a big-- my father was one out of eight, so my father's family was really a big family and the second generation's children and nephews and nieces and cousins and aunts and uncles.
And my mother's side was not quite as big, but also a big family.
Unfortunately, only a few of us survived.
Well, we were just living our immediate family before 1939.
After '39, it was another story.
Things started getting crowded and people had to double up and take in friends and relatives, mostly relatives.
But up till the beginning of the war, we lived all by ourselves in the house.
Well, belonging to these organizations I mentioned before, which consisted of going to meetings and lectures and speeches and instructions.
Back in 1939, I was chosen to-- to go to an overnight camp for four years.
And this was a camp for instructors, for the future leaders.
And it was one of the famous resort places in southern Poland, Zakopane, Z-A-K-O-P-A-N-E.
And there we have spent the entire time being instructed and also enjoying the-- the location, which was different than where we were born or raised.
It was a real treat.
But little did we realize that this was so close to what is to be happening.
I returned at the end of August and September the 1st, the war broke out.
It was too much of a shock, naturally.
Well, I an a year and a half older than my brother and my sister.
I was born in '22, my brother was born in '24, my sister was born in '26.
Since I was born.
Since I remember.
This house belonged to my mother's father.
It was a big house.
It wasn't the kind of houses we perceive here today.
It was a house with different buildings with one main entrance and inside there was a courtyard.
And within the courtyard, you have apartment buildings, two-story apartment buildings.
So there was-- this house, so-called house, housed about-- about 10 families maybe.
And due to the fact that it was my grandfather's house at one time, so after they have died, it was taken over by the successors, by my mother and my father.
We were one of the partners, so to speak, and the rest of the family.
Each one occupied an apartment.
And then there were some tenants that paid rent to us.
And we lived in that house-- I have a picture of this house that was taken it 1974.
It was-- is no more.
It was not too spacious of a house, but we loved it.
This was all we knew.
It was rather primitive compared with today's standards.
There was no water and no-- there was electricity, but there was an outhouse, there was-- water had to be fetched from the outside.
It wasn't easy, but we didn't-- we were happy with it and so was everybody else.
This is all we knew and this is we have enjoyed.
Thinking back, it's just unbelievable how happy we were.
Of course, we were much younger and life that has passed us has been-- we missed, of course.
But we liked it.
We liked it.

Of course, we have encountered anti-Semitism on every step of the road, but due to the fact that this house had-- this city was strictly populated by Jews, the Jewish population of the city before the war was somewhere between 27,000 and 29,000 people.
Its council and its committees and everything was run according to the rules and regulations prevailing at the time and in accordance with the Polish regulations, naturally.

We may not have felt the anti-Semites right on the street where we lived because it was all Jewish people, but as soon as you ventured outside of the outskirts of the city, you could not miss the point that anti-Semitism is prevailing and so forth and so on.
Yes.
Like for instance, in the summertime when we would take trips out of town, we'd hire these-- the organization would take us out for outings, picnics.
We would be stoned on the way.
This was a natural thing.
We would have been surprised if it wasn't the case.
Of course, we didn't like it.
And in many cases, we fought back but it was a fact of life that we lived with.
And you just couldn't not notice it and you couldn't avoid it.
Well, in 1939, right from the very beginning.
When the war first broke out, things were turned upside-down.
But we still did not realize how much-- how what we are entering.
We couldn't anticipate it because it was just unbelievable that these things would happen.
Of course, there were too many rumors flying around and nothing could be substantiated, but we knew that the war broke out.

And two days later, the next day, we saw the German front tanks marching through our streets due to the fact that our street was so close to the German border and this happened to have been one of the main thoroughfares.
Right the street where I was born and raised, right in front of the house, they were coming from-- from the west, marching east.
And for hours without interruption we saw these motorcycles.
They had leaders and then all the heavy armory and artillery and whatever.
And this went on for a whole day and a whole night.
And all the-- all the people-- citizens would stay out there at the sidewalks and watch like it was a parade, but we very well know it wasn't a parade.
It was a-- a-- a something that's what we could--we could sense it that something terrible was going to happen, but we just didn't know what, when.
And we just stood there for hours at a time.
I remember it like it was yesterday, watching all this-- the-- the organized disciplined German Army.
It was frightening.
It was frightening to see.
And a week later, only one week later, they have bombed, threw incinerators into the synagogue, main synagogue, and we only lived a short while from there.

And there was a big synagogue, the main synagogue-- there were hundreds of synagogues in our town, but there was one main synagogue which was a beautiful building that was renovated shortly before the war broke out.
And it was the pride, it was the crown of the Jewish citizenry, of the Jewish people.
Ironically, that big synagogue was not too far from the church, from the big church.
And then there was a castle, the Zamaek, Z-A-M-A-E-K, which is a Polish name for a castle.
This castle predates to the 14th century.
You have all these Polish heroes and wars and kings.
That castle is still there to this day.
Of course, it's a ruin and of course, they've renovated and so forth.
But this castle is a historic castle.
This castle was within a catcall from the synagogue and the church is not far from there.
Whatever non-Jewish people lived in Bendin, this was their church.
The week after the Germans marched in, they encircled the entire area of the synagogue and they had bombed and burned and have shot everybody who tried to escape.
This was an inferno a week after they marched in.
Everybody naturally went into hiding.
We, living not too far from there, we could peek out and see flames engulfing, shooting up to the heavens for an entire day and night.
This happened on a weekend.
We didn't realize to what extent this-- this-- this tragic thing took place, we didn't understand.
But on a Sunday morning, I couldn't be hidden any longer, I was too restless.
And I snuck out on the street and as we snuck out-- with a few other youngsters, naturally-- but as the fire subsided, we wanted to see what has happened.
We knew something terrible happened.
And we walked out and we walked and we saw nothing but burned ruins, and we saw burned people.

And immediately a committee organized, we walked around with these burlap sacks and we up and the charred bodies, hands and parts of bodies, and hauled them away to a cemetery to give them a decent burial.
And then as days went on, we realized how many people are missing.
A whole city, practically a whole little city within a city, all these poor people were shot as they tried to escape.
Now, later on it came out that some people escaped into the church and a priest, whoever was in charge, did hide some people and they were saved that night.
But hundreds-- literally hundreds-- of people were shot within the fires.
And this was just like a-- like a foreplay as to what is going to happen.
Well, we didn't have any Polish neighbors.
But they were standing-- whatever Polish people were living there, they were indifferent to the result.
I think they were staying inside and they were-- they did not react at all.
We don't know of any, other than the church people, that did let a few in.
This came out, incidentally, not too long ago, a couple of years ago, when I went back to Poland to revisit, this also came to my knowledge.
But the reaction of the Polish people was very indifferent.
Yes.
Yes.
We-- we have known a lot of people who were, to our shock and surprise, realized that they are no more.
It was like a big-- a big fire-- a big Holocaust within that section, within that area.
It wasn't the whole city, it was this area which was the main center of Jewish houses of worship and of Jewish living.
Of course, this place was so densely populated-- people lived in cellars, people lived in attics, raised families.
And on a Saturday or a holiday, you could see how people came out of their-- of the-- of these halls and how many people lived in those-- in this particular area.
All this was just burned down to the ground within two days and everybody that tried to escape was shot.
What happened next?
Little by little, there were new rules and regulations, these big-- what do you call them-- announcements, all kinds of restrictions.
Jews are not to-- allowed to do this, Jews are not allowed to do that.
Curfew was inaugurated.
By eight o'clock, you had to be within your house.
You could not travel to any places.
Slowly, they were trying to restrict our movements.
Like there was now immediately a council was organized, Germans saw to it that the councils were organized.
And that council actually governed the day-to-day life in the city.
There were new-- new order, new edicts issued.
Naturally, everybody had to start getting a card.
Food started getting rationed.
So you had to get coupons, you had to get a card from the council.
Committees were formed and the Germans all through this here walked day in and day out the neighborhoods and were just shooting people for no reason at all.
The slightest transgression and you were shot.
And we could see this here, so naturally, anybody they thought who would try to escape or do some funny things had a second thought.
We realized how serious the situation is and how tragic it is.
And hoping and praying that someday this is going to stop.
But instead, things started getting from bad to worse.
It was not a free election.
The Germans appointed whatever.
It was former-- former Jewish community active people that were put on and then a host of other people who, by their own initiative, or by their own doings, became leaders.
And those leaders later on in the later years-- in '41, '42, '43-- they became kings because they-- they were deciding upon death and life.
The Germans have requested from them to deliver so many Jews per month or whatever.
And naturally, they made up the lists and they were the ones who sent out the Jewish militia, which was formed by the council to pick up these people.
And of course, there were all kinds of ways of getting out of it.
First of all, everybody had to be working and have an ID card.
That ID card meant being alive or else not being alive.
So people tried to falsify ID cards and got them by hook or crook.
But the main object of [INAUDIBLE] was if you were working in a shop which was organized by the Germans for their own advantages.
But they needed people to work so they organized these groups.
And these shops provided them with an ID cards as being useful to work.
Like the fact that we survived together, not just for so long, till the very end was that we, the whole family, had cards and we worked in this shoe factory.
And this saved us.
There was also other shops.
There was a tailor shop which was famous.
And by the way, the people that run those shops were Germans, but they were not as-- their job was to see that the work is being done properly and that production is being done.
But they were not actually treating us badly.
But it's through the employment that we survived to be living together.
Otherwise, every couple of months the Jewish [GERMAN], which is the German word for the Jewish council, was given an order to deliver so many Jews.
Now, these-- these Jews, mostly young people, were sent to a work camp.
At that time, they were not concentration camps.
The concentration camps came into existence in '42 or '43 when the Nazis-- the SS took over.
They were in charge of them.
But before, they were actually done by the German authorities, by the occupation authorities.
These people were picked up from the street and hauled away by truck to certain points and then they were shipped out to camps.
The camps were hard labor, very meager food.
And they were never seen by their families.
In some cases at the very beginning, some of them were able to smuggle out-- the restrictions weren't so tight.
But how did they get these people?
They get these people-- they had-- they-- they actually grabbed them on the street or else there were lists and they were notified so and so has to report to such and such.
And then if you didn't report, they came after you and they made you report.
Naturally, we were lucky.
My father and my brother and myself, we were working in this factory, which was another street.
This factory was a known big factory, shoe factory, owned by the people, ironically, in the same name of our own but there was no relationship.
We worked there from 1940, '41.
And after work, we came home, eight o'clock you had to be in the house.
There was no radio.
Anybody being caught with a radio was shot.
And this happened every day, so naturally we were very frightened.
But I'll tell you this much, we heard people being shot in the street at eight o'clock who came in late.
One time I had an incident, how I escaped was just God was with me.
I came home five minutes late, I was too energetic and too active to submit myself to these restrictions.
However painful they were, you try to be a wise guy sometimes.
In this particular case, I came home about five minutes after.
I came home, I ran.
And I saw the streets are clean and my-- my heart was pounding and I knew I was late.
I may not have known how late I was, but I was pretty close to eight o'clock.
And the streets are empty.
And as I'm about a half a block away from my house running, this SS man screams, halt, stop.
And I raised my hands right away and he has this gun and he says, do you know what time it is?
And I says, I live right here.
Do you know what time it is?
He slapped me a couple of times, he kicked me, and he let me go.
But the days before, the days after I could hear one time right behind our windows-- we lived on the second floor-- behind our windows I could hear one was shot right in front of the window.
I could hear his-- his dying-- his dying sounds and how depressing, how tragic, how frightened we were.
We were just squeezing ourselves one to another and we just couldn't believe that this is happening to us.
So what happened?
We worked every day, we came home.
Food was scarce, everything was rationed.
You had to get your bread by coupons and the coupons you got through the working card and so forth and so on.
There was a big black market, naturally, and people sold their whatever, their possessions they had to feed themselves and other things.

Yet the Jewish Council, on the Jewish committee, the Jewish gemeide-- gemeide is a German word, G-E-M-E-I-D-E-- the Jewish gemeide, Yiddish gemeide, they tried to organize schools, they tried to make life a little bit more bearable.
But of course, it was all under the auspices of the German government and they-- whatever the Jewish gemeide tried to do, they just knocked it down.
And the main problemm-- the main function of this Jewish gemede was to deliver the Jewish people to the Germans.
You must not forget one thing, that the smaller villages and towns surrounding this big city of Bendin were cleaned out and everything was pushed into the big city.
And it's happening in every part of Poland and every part of the German [GERMAN].
First they cleaned up the smaller people and then they squeezed them into the-- so it was the Jewish gemeide, the Jewish council's job to locate, to accommodate those people.
So living quarters started getting tight.
People were squeezed together and food--
